cataloging_subject.label: Early English Artistical Receipts (Wright's 1844 selection from Sloane MS 73)
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the exact editorial selection of twelve Middle English technical recipe or practical-advice units that Thomas Wright printed from British Library Sloane MS 73 on pp. 152-155 of the Archaeological Journal in 1844 and that U-M encoded as CME00136. Wright drew them from six discontinuous manuscript loci and printed the loci out of folio order: ff. 173v, 138v, 138r, 213r, 213v, and 215v. The subject is not one securely bounded medieval authored work, not every craft recipe in Sloane MS 73, not Wright's modern-English introduction and notes, and not the reconstructed Trinity Encyclopedia. The manuscript witness, 1844 article, completed 1845 bound carrier, scan, U-M encoding, repository XML, and later scholarship remain separate lineage layers.

catalog_summary.title: Early English Artistical Receipts (Wright's 1844 selection from Sloane MS 73)
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous
catalog_summary.date: Exact composition dates unknown; attested in a fifteenth-century witness and indexed by MED as before 1475; first known publication of this exact selection in 1844
catalog_summary.region: England; exact origins unknown; only the hand containing target f. 138r-v is associated by MEG-C/LALME with Bedfordshire

titles.value: Early English Artistical Receipts (Wright's 1844 selection from Sloane MS 73)

assertion.notes: The witness, editor, and year qualifier distinguishes this exact discontinuous selection from other craft recipes in Sloane MS 73 and from the related Trinity Encyclopedia; it is not a manuscript title.

notes: This is Wright's printed heading, not evidence for an original manuscript title or rubric.

scope.description: The first medieval unit in Wright's printed order

titles.value: the more fyn of colour wole it be whanne it is al maad

scope.description: The ending of the final medieval unit in Wright's printed order

agents.name: Thomas Wright
notes: The article is signed T. WRIGHT. He is the modern editor and probable transcriber, not a medieval author.

scope.description: The twelve constituent recipe and advice units, which need not share one author

assertion.notes: Anonymous is a display responsibility, not a claim that one unidentified person composed all twelve units.

scope.description: The complete four-page 1844 printed selection and its modern apparatus

assertion.notes: The closing signature T. WRIGHT. confirms responsibility for the article; the exact degree of normalization and intervention remains unresolved.

scope.description: The selected manuscript readings printed from Sloane MS 73

assertion.notes: Wright says the receipts are taken from Sloane MS 73, but does not document whether he worked directly from the codex or through an intermediary transcript.

date_statements.display: Unknown; the twelve constituent units may have different origins and may predate their surviving witness

assertion.notes: Witness dating and textual parallels do not establish composition dates for the individual recipes.

date_statements.display: Fifteenth century; MED indexes Wright's selected text as (a1475), while Wright described the manuscript as written in the earlier part of the fifteenth century

scope.description: The selected units' survival in Sloane MS 73, not a confirmed date for one autonomous compilation

assertion.notes: The current British Library record's 0900-1799 date range applies to the whole composite and cannot date these target folios. MED's a1475 source date and Wright's earlier-fifteenth-century description are preserved as distinct scholarly assertions.

notes: Individual recipes or textual traditions may be older than their Sloane witness.

date_statements.display: 1844 (first known publication of this exact twelve-unit selection)

assertion.notes: The completed bound volume has a separate 1845 title-page imprint; that carrier date does not replace the article and journal-volume year 1844.

places.label: Bedfordshire, England
notes: Used only for MEG-C/LALME code L4708, the hand spanning Sloane MS 73 ff. 95v-146 and therefore target f. 138r-v.

assertion.notes: The selected texts are Middle English and circulate in an English manuscript context; no narrower common place of composition or compilation is established.

scope.description: Only the two target units on f. 138r-v, within the hand MEG-C defines as ff. 95v-146

assertion.notes: This is a scribal-language association. It is not transferred to f. 173v, ff. 213r-v, f. 215v, authorial origin, or the place where Wright formed his selection.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: Modern English in Wright's heading, introduction, folio labels, footnotes, and signature belongs to the 1844 editorial layer and is not a language facet of the twelve-unit medieval cataloging subject.

form_statements.description: All twelve medieval units are prose recipes or practical advice. The XML contains no intrinsic verse groups or lines.

assertion.notes: The six folio loci are printed discontinuously and out of manuscript folio order.

summaries.value: Twelve practical prose units explain how to color wax red and green; manufacture vermilion, verdigris, white lead, and red lead; multiply verdigris; soften glass; prepare azure from lapis lazuli; distinguish true lapis lazuli from lapis almanie by visual and heat tests; and make azure bice from white lead and the juice of a blue cornfield flower. The recipes emphasize proportions, vessels, heat, manure incubation, repeated processing, signs of completion, and tests of material quality.

Wright prints the medieval material in six folio-labeled groups containing 2, 1, 1, 3, 1, and 4 units respectively. CME00136 has seven DIV2 elements because a modern-English editorial introduction precedes those six document groups. Its 21 P elements comprise one introduction paragraph, six folio-headnote paragraphs, and fourteen medieval content paragraphs; the vermilion and f. 138r verdigris recipes each use separate rubric and instruction paragraphs, so fourteen content paragraphs represent twelve recipe/advice units rather than fourteen works.

The record models Wright's exact selection as an edited selection and retains twelve internal units for description and concordance. It does not infer that the printed order reproduces a continuous manuscript sequence, transfer Sloane 73's whole-codex date range to the target folios, treat the related Trinity Encyclopedia as a physical source, or count Wright's introduction, twelve footnotes, marginal vessel figure, or six folio headnotes as medieval works.

extent.description: The cataloged medieval selection contains twelve unnumbered prose recipe/advice units at six discontinuous folio loci. In the XML these appear beneath six document DIV2s, plus a seventh DIV2 for Wright's editorial introduction. Fourteen medieval content P elements represent twelve units because the f. 138v vermilion and f. 138r verdigris units each split a rubric from its procedural paragraph. Six HEADNOTE/P elements supply folio labels and one further P is Wright's introduction, yielding 21 P elements overall. The selection occupies four printed pages, pp. 152-155.

content_parts.label: Thomas Wright's editorial introduction
content_parts.summary: A modern-English paragraph identifies Sloane MS 73, dates it to the earlier fifteenth century, compares its color recipes with documents printed in the journal's first number, and singles out the glass-softening recipe as curious.

locators.value: p. 152, opening paragraph below EARLY ENGLISH ARTISTICAL RECEIPTS

notes: Editorial paratext, not one of the twelve medieval items and excluded from intrinsic-work counts and language facets.

content_parts.label: Sloane MS 73, f. 173v: colored wax
content_parts.summary: Two recipes for red and green wax using white wax, turpentine, and vermilion or verdigris.

locators.scheme: manuscript folio as printed by Wright

locators.value: PDF page 170 / Internet Archive leaf n172

content_parts.label: For to make reed wex
content_parts.summary: Mixes white wax and turpentine, tests the gumminess in cold water, and colors the melted mixture with finely ground vermilion.

locators.scheme: manuscript folio as printed by Wright

content_parts.label: For to make grene wex
content_parts.summary: Adapts the red-wax method by coloring the wax and turpentine mixture with ground verdigris.

locators.scheme: manuscript folio as printed by Wright

content_parts.label: Sloane MS 73, f. 138v: vermilion
content_parts.summary: One long two-paragraph recipe for manufacturing vermilion from quicksilver and brimstone, with furnace directions, color signs, and safety advice.

locators.scheme: manuscript folio as printed by Wright

locators.value: PDF pages 170-171 / Internet Archive leaves n172-n173

content_parts.label: Here it techith how thou schalt make good vermyloun
content_parts.summary: Combines quicksilver and brimstone, describes enclosed heating and sublimation in a luted glass jordan, uses changes in flame color to judge progress, and warns the maker to eat and drink before encountering the harmful fumes.

locators.scheme: manuscript folio as printed by Wright

notes: The two P elements are one rubric-plus-procedure unit, not two recipes. Wright's footnotes a-c and the reproduced marginal jordan figure are editorial apparatus.

content_parts.label: Sloane MS 73, f. 138r: verdigris
content_parts.summary: One two-paragraph recipe for making verdigris from copper filings and vinegar in a sealed vessel surrounded by horse dung.

locators.scheme: manuscript folio as printed by Wright

locators.value: PDF page 171 / Internet Archive leaf n173

content_parts.label: Here it techith how thou schalt make fyn vertgrece and good
content_parts.summary: Specifies copper-to-vinegar proportions and two months of sealed incubation in a measured surround of horse dung.

locators.scheme: manuscript folio as printed by Wright

notes: The two P elements are one rubric-plus-procedure unit, not two recipes.

content_parts.label: Sloane MS 73, f. 213r: lead and copper pigments
content_parts.summary: Three recipes for white lead, red lead, and verdigris.

locators.scheme: manuscript folio as printed by Wright

locators.value: PDF page 172 / Internet Archive leaf n174

content_parts.label: For to make whit leed
content_parts.summary: Suspends new lead plates above red-wine lees in a sealed vessel kept in hot horse dung, then scrapes, washes, dries, and optionally forms the white-lead product into saleable pieces.

locators.value: f. 213r; Clarke MS S section C7

notes: Clarke identifies the opening of Sloane section C7 as a very compressed version corresponding to Trinity section 1. This is a textual counterpart, not evidence that Sloane MS 73 copied directly from the surviving Trinity College manuscript.

content_parts.label: Now for to make reed leed
content_parts.summary: Grinds dried white lead to powder and heats it in an earthen pot with repeated stirring until the desired red color appears.

locators.scheme: manuscript folio as printed by Wright

content_parts.label: For to make vertegrece
content_parts.summary: Suspends copper plates over vinegar or strong lees in a warm sealed vessel, repeatedly scrapes the green product, grinds it with vinegar, and presses and dries it in leather.

locators.scheme: manuscript folio as printed by Wright

content_parts.label: Sloane MS 73, f. 213v: multiplying verdigris
content_parts.summary: One recipe for repeatedly increasing prepared verdigris with copper filings and strong vinegar.

locators.scheme: manuscript folio as printed by Wright

locators.value: PDF page 173 / Internet Archive leaf n175

content_parts.label: To multiplie vertegrece
content_parts.summary: Mixes Spanish verdigris with copper filings and strong vinegar, incubates the sealed mixture in hot horse dung, and repeats the process with fresh filings and vinegar.

locators.scheme: manuscript folio as printed by Wright

content_parts.label: Sloane MS 73, f. 215v: glass and azure
content_parts.summary: Four units on softening glass, preparing azure from lapis lazuli without paste, recognizing and testing lapis lazuli, and making azure bice.

locators.scheme: manuscript folio as printed by Wright

locators.value: PDF page 173 / Internet Archive leaf n175

content_parts.label: For to make glas nesche
content_parts.summary: Boils glass with warmed goat's blood and mustard juice so that it becomes soft and resists breaking when thrown against a wall.

locators.scheme: manuscript folio as printed by Wright

content_parts.label: For to make fyn azure withoute past
content_parts.summary: Dissolves sal ammoniac, heats and quenches whole lapis lazuli, washes away the salt, and dries the resulting color.

locators.scheme: manuscript folio as printed by Wright

content_parts.label: How to recognize and assay lapis lazuli
content_parts.summary: Describes true lapis lazuli by its fine blue color and gold-like streaks or specks, rejects visibly gravelly stones, and distinguishes it from lapis almanie by heating, cooling, color, hardness, and brittleness.

locators.scheme: manuscript folio as printed by Wright

notes: The supplied English label describes an unheaded advice and testing unit; it is not presented as a manuscript rubric.

content_parts.label: On this manere thu myȝt make azure bis
content_parts.summary: Repeatedly grinds white lead or ceruse with the juice of a blue flower growing in corn, drying between grindings until the desired blue color is reached.

locators.scheme: manuscript folio as printed by Wright

related_works.title: The Trinity Encyclopedia
notes: Clarke's 2018 concordance identifies the target white-lead unit on Sloane f. 213r as MS S section C7 corresponding to Trinity Encyclopedia MS T section 1, and calls the beginning of the Sloane version very compressed.

The relationship is asserted only for part:white-lead. No copying direction is established, the broader twelve-unit selection is not equated with the Trinity Encyclopedia, and Trinity College MS O.9.39 is not modeled as Sloane MS 73's physical source ancestor.

evidence.locator: TEMPHEAD; IDG; complete TEXT and element audit
evidence.summary: Identifies CME00136 and preserves Wright's complete four-page article text: modern introduction, six folio-labeled document groups, twelve medieval recipe/advice units in fourteen content paragraphs, twelve footnotes, one vessel figure description, four page breaks, and signed closer T. WRIGHT.

evidence.citation: Linked lineage evidence from Thomas Wright's 1844 Archaeological Journal article and complete bound-volume scan.
evidence.summary: Supports the exact printed selection, article heading and signature, six manuscript loci, twelve-unit boundaries, readings, editorial introduction and notes, pp. 152-155 boundary, and the distinct article-year and bound-title-page date layers.

evidence.citation: Linked lineage evidence from the British Library record for Sloane MS 73.
evidence.summary: Supports shelfmark, record ID, MDARK, whole-composite contents and date range, languages, script, extent as one item, custodial history, access route, and the empty digitised-content field. The whole-record dates are not applied to the target folios.

evidence.citation: Linked lineage evidence from the Middle English Dictionary source stencil and target quotations for Recipe Painting(2).
evidence.summary: Supports the source designation (a1475) Recipe Painting(2) in Archaeol.J.1 (Sln 73), exact pp. 152-155 quotation locators, and the need to keep MED's separate ff. 196-202 hand note from the target folios.

evidence.citation: Linked lineage evidence from MEG-C Catalogue of Sources, version 2011.1, entry L4708.
evidence.summary: Associates the single scribal text spanning Sloane MS 73 ff. 95v-146 with Bedfordshire, date 15b1/a1475, and Cursiva anglicana media. Only target f. 138r-v lies in that stated hand range.

evidence.citation: Linked lineage evidence from Mark Clarke, Tricks of the Medieval Trades (2018), official publisher sample.
evidence.summary: Supplies the T-S concordance and the precise target relationship MS T section 1 = MS S section C7, identifies the Sloane white-lead opening as very compressed, and supports a qualified textual counterpart rather than physical copying direction.

evidence.citation: Linked lineage evidence from Trinity College Cambridge's catalog and IIIF manifest for O.9.39.
evidence.summary: Identifies the counterpart physical manuscript and its Part I book of mostly color and dye receipts, complete public IIIF route, recapture date, and CC BY-NC 4.0 provider license. It does not establish that O.9.39 was Sloane MS 73's exemplar.

evidence.citation: Linked lineage, access, rights, and fixity evidence for CME00136.
evidence.summary: Supports the U-M identity, source-edition relationship, complete journal carrier and page mapping, cached source fixity, dated negative target-manuscript image finding, onsite consultation route, reproduction-order route, and separate rights layers.

open_questions.question: What are the exact starts, ends, neighboring texts, hands, and original sequence positions of all twelve units on Sloane MS 73 ff. 138r-v, 173v, 213r-v, and 215v?
next_steps: Inspect complete color images of every target recto and verso plus adjacent surfaces.
Build a line-by-line concordance against Wright and record whether rubrics and boundaries are manuscript features.
Do not infer continuity from Wright's compact four-page presentation or treat the broad current BL contents ranges as target extent.

open_questions.question: When and where were the individual recipe units composed and copied, and how many scribal hands are represented across the six loci?
next_steps: Keep Wright's earlier-fifteenth-century statement and MED's a1475 indexing as separate source assertions.
Apply MEG-C's Bedfordshire L4708 association only to the hand spanning ff. 95v-146 and therefore target f. 138r-v.
Consult complete images, current codicological descriptions, IMEP records, and recipe scholarship before dating or localizing the other target folios.

open_questions.question: Which of the other eleven target units correspond to Trinity Encyclopedia sections or other craft-recipe traditions, and what direction or stemmatic relationship, if any, can be established?
next_steps: Retain only Clarke's secure target correspondence between Sloane C7 and Trinity section 1 in the current record.
Consult Clarke's complete 2018 concordance and 2016 edition for target-specific section mappings and variants.
Model textual versions or parallels separately from surviving physical manuscripts and do not infer direct copying without evidence.

open_questions.question: What normalization, punctuation, expansion, omission, reordering, or silent correction did Wright apply to the Sloane readings?
next_steps: Collate Wright's text, rubrics, folio labels, notes, and vessel figure directly against manuscript images.
Investigate the printed 'etc.' in the fine-azure recipe without assuming it marks manuscript text, omission, or damage.
Do not treat U-M's faithful encoding of print as independent verification of Wright's readings.

open_questions.question: What exact issue date and issue-wrapper history produced the 1844 article within a completed volume whose bound title page is dated 1845?
next_steps: Consult original issue wrappers, publisher records, and authoritative serial bibliography.
Continue to record article and journal year 1844 separately from bound-carrier title-page year 1845.
Do not collapse the two date layers into a synthesized single publication date.

notes: The preferred title is supplied for disambiguation; Wright's all-capital printed heading is retained separately.

The twelve medieval units, fourteen medieval content paragraphs, six document DIV2s, seven total DIV2s, six folio loci, and four printed pages are distinct metrics and are not substituted for one another.
The current British Library record is a broad interim whole-composite description. Its 10th-18th-century range and listed contents do not isolate the target craft recipes.

The MED bibliography note for Sloane 73 ff. 196-202 concerns another hand and source stream; its only target use here is the explicit cross-reference to (a1475) Recipe Painting(2) in Archaeological Journal 1.

A complete target-manuscript facsimile should cover the entire current physical Sloane MS 73 object and map the twelve selected units separately; target-leaf crops alone would not constitute complete manuscript coverage.
